items.title: Tuberculin test and Quantiferon
items.contentHtml:
Tuberculin test and Quantiferon

  • Tuberculin test (PPD or Mantoux): reading at 48-72 h; induration is measured, not erythema. Cut-off point: ≥5 mm in immunocompromised/contacts, ≥10 mm in other risk groups, ≥15 mm in people without risk factors. False negatives due to anergy (human immunodeficiency virus, measles, recent vaccination with live viruses).
  • Quantiferon-TB Gold (IGRA): measures the release of IFN-γ after stimulation with ESAT-6, CFP-10, TB7.7 antigens. Advantage: not crossed with BCG or non-tuberculous mycobacteria (except M. kansasii, M. szulgai, M. marinum). Indicated in patients vaccinated with BCG or with previous false positives of PPD.
  • Differential diagnosis: Positive PPD does not distinguish latent infection from active disease; always complement with clinic and chest x-ray.

items.title: Bacilloscopy with Ziehl-Neelsen stain
items.contentHtml:
Baciloscopy with Ziehl-Neelsen stain

  • Ziehl-Neelsen stain: uses basic fuchsin with heat (phenol) and decolorization with alcohol-acid; Mycobacteria retain the dye due to their wall rich in mycolic acids. Result: red bacilli on a blue background (contrast with methylene blue).
  • Sensitivity: 10⁴ bacilli/mL of sputum. At least 2 samples (morning expectoration) are required for diagnosis. High specificity, but does not differentiateM. tuberculosisof other mycobacteria.
  • Trap examination: negative smear microscopy does not rule out tuberculosis; in paucibacillary forms (children, human immunodeficiency virus, extrapulmonary TB) the sensitivity falls to 30-50%. It correlates with the degree of positivity (1+ to 3+ according to WHO).

items.title: Cultivation in Löwenstein-Jensen medium
items.contentHtml:
Culture in Löwenstein-Jensen medium

  • Löwenstein-Jensen medium (LJ): solid medium based on egg, asparagine and malachite green (inhibits accompanying flora). Slow growth: visible colonies in 3-8 weeks (average 4-6). Rough, creamy colonies, “breadcrumb” color.
  • Alternative: liquid medium Middlebrook 7H9 in automated systems (MGIT) reduces time to 10-14 days. Culture is the gold standard for diagnosis and allows drug sensitivity testing.
  • Key fact: requires incubation at 37°C with 5-10% CO₂. The sensitivity is 10-100 bacilli/mL, much higher than smear microscopy. Growth time should be reported to guide empirical treatment.

items.title: PCR for DNA detection
items.contentHtml:
PCR for DNA detection

  • Xpert MTB/RIF (GeneXpert): Real-time PCR that detects DNA from M. tuberculosisand mutations in the rpoB gene (associated with resistance to rifampicin). Sensitivity >95% in positive smear and ~70% in negative. Result in 2 hours.
  • WHO indication: first diagnostic test in suspected pulmonary and extrapulmonary TB, especially in human immunodeficiency virus+ and children. Also in resistant TB for rapid detection of resistance to rifampicin (substitute marker for MDR).
  • Limitation: does not differentiate viable from non-viable DNA; may give false positives after recent treatment. It does not replace culture for complete sensitivity.

items.title: Chest and cavern x-ray
items.contentHtml:
chest X-ray of the chest and caverns

  • Classic finding: infiltrates in the upper lobes (apexes) with caverns (thick walls >2 mm, air content). The caverns are pathognomonic of active TB in clinical context.
  • Other findings: centrilobular nodules in “tree in bud”, hilar/mediastinal lymphadenopathy (more common in children and human immunodeficiency virus), pleural effusion (unilateral, lymphocytic exudate).
  • Differential diagnosis: caverns also in lung abscess (air-fluid level), cavitated cancer (irregular wall, satellite nodule), granulomatosis with polyangiitis. Normal chest x-ray does not rule out TB (especially in human immunodeficiency virus with severe immunosuppression).

items.foco: Rifampicin and isoniazid as first line
items.title: Rifampicin and isoniazid as first line
items.contentHtml:
Rifampin and isoniazid as first line

  • Isoniazid (INH): dose 5 mg/kg/day (max 300 mg) or 15 mg/kg 3 times/week. Bactericidal against actively replicating bacilli; It acts by inhibiting the synthesis of mycolic acids. High initial potency.
  • Rifampicin (RIF): dose 10 mg/kg/day (max 600 mg). Bactericidal against all bacillary populations (including intracellular and slow growing). Inhibits DNA-dependent RNA polymerase. Strong CYP450 inducer.
  • Standard regimen: 2-month intensive phase with isoniazid, rifampicin, pyrazinamide and ethambutol; then continuation phase 4 months with isoniazid and rifampicin. The combination prevents resistance.

items.foco: Resistance to isoniazid due to katG mutation
items.title: Resistance to isoniazid due to katG mutation
items.contentHtml:
Isoniazid resistance due to katG mutation

  • Main mechanism: mutation in the katG gene (encodes catalase-peroxidase) that prevents the activation of isoniazid to its active form. Responsible for 50-80% of INH resistance.
  • Most frequent mutation: S315T (serine for threonine in codon 315) confers high resistance without complete loss of catalase function, allowing bacterial survival.
  • Other mutations: in the inhA gene (enol-ACP reductase) confer low-level resistance. and sometimes cross resistance with ethionamide. PCR detection (Xpert MTB/XDR) guides treatment.

items.title: Directly Observed Treatment Shortened (DOTS)
items.contentHtml:
Directly Observed Short-course Treatment (DOTS)

  • DOTS (Directly Observed Therapy, Short-course): WHO strategy that combines 5 components: government commitment, smear diagnosis, supervised standardized treatment, regular drug supply and registration/monitoring system.
  • Supervision: a health professional observes the intake of each dose, especially in the intensive phase. It reduces the dropout rate and prevents the appearance of acquired resistance.
  • Exam trap: DOTS is not just “taking pills in front of someone”; includes logistical support and guarantee of adherence. Directly observed therapy (DOT) is only one component of DOTS.

items.title: Adverse effect of rifampicin: hepatotoxicity
items.contentHtml:
Adverse effect of rifampicin: hepatotoxicity

  • Hepatotoxicity: rifampicin can cause elevation of transaminases (ALT/AST) and, in severe cases, cholestatic hepatitis or liver necrosis. It is more common in combination with isoniazide (hepatotoxic synergy).
  • Monitoring: measure liver enzymes at baseline and monthly. Discontinue if ALT >3 times the upper limit of normal with symptoms (jaundice, nausea, abdominal pain) or >5 times without symptoms.
  • Management: remove hepatotoxic drugs, evaluate gradual restart. Rifampicin is less hepatotoxic than isoniazid; it is often reintroduced first.

items.title: Isoniazid neuropathy and B6 prophylaxis
items.contentHtml:
Neuropathy due to isoniazid and prophylaxis with B6

  • Peripheral neuropathy: dose-dependent adverse effect of isoniazid (more common with doses >5 mg/kg/day or in patients with risk factors: diabetes, alcoholism, malnutrition, pregnancy, human immunodeficiency virus).
  • Mechanism: isoniazide interferes with the metabolism of pyridoxine (vitamin B6) by forming inactive hydrazones, causing functional deficiency of B6 necessary for the synthesis of neurotransmitters.
  • Prophylaxis: administer pyridoxine (B6) 25-50 mg/day in all patients with risk factors or high doses of isoniazide. Neuropathy is reversible if detected early and supplemented.
